planting.note: Plant in late spring when soil and air are warm; Weeping Fig is sensitive to cold (leaf drop below 40°F) and should not be planted outdoors until all danger of cold nights has passed. In zone 9a, plant in the most protected microclimate available — south or east facing walls, urban heat pockets, or under structural overhangs. In zone 9b and warmer, it can be planted as a landscape specimen with minimal frost protection.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, fertile soil; in zone 9, afternoon shade protection extends the plant's cold hardiness and prevents leaf scorch during heat waves. Ficus benjamina is the world's most widely cultivated indoor tree and one of the most popular tropical landscape trees in zone 9b–11 Gulf Coast, Florida, and California landscapes, where mature specimens with their gracefully arching, weeping branches laden with small, glossy, pointed leaves create an elegant, tropical canopy. In warm climates, trees grow to 50–60 feet with an equally broad spreading crown. The smooth gray trunk often develops aerial roots in humid conditions. The plant produces small, inedible figs in zone 10-11 but rarely fruits in zone 9. Ficus benjamina is notorious indoors for dropping leaves when conditions change — any shift in light, temperature, humidity, or watering schedule triggers a stress response. For outdoor landscape use in zone 9b, choose a permanent location as transplanting established trees causes significant leaf drop. The aggressive root system can damage sidewalks, foundations, and underground utilities within 20-30 feet; avoid planting near hardscape or infrastructure. A spectacular specimen when given room and a frost-free site.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ficus benjamina (Weeping Fig)
California Cooperative Extension — Ficus species for urban landscapes
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Ficus benjamina
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tropical Trees for South Texas Landscapes

common_name: Indian Laurel (Chinese Banyan, Laurel Fig, Cuban Laurel)

planting.note: Plant in late spring in a permanent location with adequate space — Indian Laurel develops an enormous spreading canopy and aggressive surface root system; space at least 20–30 feet from sidewalks, driveways, foundations, and utilities. More cold-hardy than Ficus benjamina; established trees can tolerate brief temperatures to 28°F. 'Green Island' is a popular compact cultivar (6–8 feet) with no invasive root problems, suitable for foundation planting.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in any well-drained soil; Indian Laurel is broadly adaptable to soil types but the aggressive surface roots require careful placement away from pavement and structures. Ficus microcarpa (Indian Laurel, Chinese Banyan, or Cuban Laurel) is the Ficus species most commonly used as a street tree in South Florida, Gulf Coast cities, and subtropical regions worldwide — lining downtown boulevards in Miami, Houston, New Orleans, and countless Gulf Coast municipalities with its massive, dense, dark-green canopy. In tropical climates, mature trees develop spectacular aerial roots that cascade from branches and eventually thicken into secondary trunks, creating the multi-trunked banyan tree form. A 150-year-old specimen can cover an acre of ground. The 'Green Island' cultivar (Ficus microcarpa 'Green Island') is a compact, slow-growing selection with small, rounded, thick leaves and a controlled habit (6–8 feet) that makes an outstanding shade-tolerant foundation or container plant without the invasive root system of the straight species. Ficus microcarpa is the primary host plant of the invasive fig gall wasp (Josephiella) and laurel fig thrips in some regions — consult local extension services before large-scale planting in natural areas.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ficus microcarpa (Indian Laurel)
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Ficus microcarpa
Miami-Dade County Urban Forestry — Ficus species landscape evaluation
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Ficus species for Gulf Coast landscapes

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in well-drained, alkaline-tolerant soil in full sun to partial shade; Dioon edule is one of the most cold-hardy cycads available, surviving brief temperatures to 15–20°F when established. Growth is extremely slow (one set of leaves per year); purchase the largest container size affordable. All parts of the plant contain cycasin and are toxic — seeds require extensive water treatment by indigenous peoples before safe consumption as food.

directions: Plant in well-drained, alkaline-tolerant, rocky or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; Dioon edule is extremely drought-tolerant once established and adapts to the thin, limestone-based soils of its native habitat. Dioon edule is a cycad (an ancient gymnosperm predating the dinosaurs) native to the dry mountainous regions of Veracruz, Tamaulipas, and Hidalgo states in Mexico, where it grows in thornscrub and deciduous forest on rocky, alkaline slopes. It is the most cold-hardy species in the genus Dioon, surviving brief temperatures to 15–20°F with established trunks, making it one of the most cold-tolerant cycads available for zone 8b Gulf Coast and Texas gardens. The stiff, pinnate, blue-green fronds emerge in annual flushes to form a graceful, palm-like rosette on a slowly developing trunk. Mature specimens produce large, barrel-shaped cones — male cones produce pollen and female cones contain starchy seeds up to 1 inch diameter that indigenous Huastec peoples have historically processed by prolonged water treatment to remove cycasin toxins before using as food flour (hence the common name 'Chestnut Dioon'). As a landscape specimen, Dioon edule is supremely low-maintenance, tolerating drought, alkaline soil, and partial shade that defeats most other ornamentals. An excellent choice for zone 9 xeriscape and rock gardens.
sources: Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Dioon edule cultivation notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cycads for South Florida
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Dioon edule for Texas landscapes
Cycad Society — Dioon edule species profile

common_name: Spineless Yucca (Soft Leaf Yucca, Giant Yucca)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in any well-drained soil; Spineless Yucca grows quickly in warm conditions and requires excellent drainage — it will rot in clay that stays wet after rain. Plant 6–10 feet from buildings and walkways to allow for mature spread. In zone 9a, plants may defoliate in hard freezes but resprout from the trunk if the growing tips are not frozen.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, sandy or rocky soil; Spineless Yucca is extremely drought-tolerant once established and thrives on neglect in appropriate conditions. Yucca elephantipes (also sold as Yucca gigantea) is a native of Guatemala, Honduras, and Mexico that has become one of the most widely planted tropical accent plants in Gulf Coast zone 9–11 landscapes and a standard large houseplant and container specimen throughout the world. Unlike most yuccas whose stiff leaf tips can pierce skin (earning the family name 'needles and thread'), the Spineless Yucca's long, dark-green, strap-like leaves have soft, flexible tips that cause no injury — making it safe around children and pets and usable in high-traffic landscape situations where spiny yuccas would be hazardous. The plant develops multiple trunks over time, each eventually producing dramatic 3–4 foot spikes of creamy-white, pendant, bell-shaped flowers. It makes an excellent large container specimen for patios, entryways, and atriums where the bold form contrasts with fine-textured plants. When planted directly in the ground in zone 9b–11, it can grow to 30 feet with multiple branching trunks and a truly impressive specimen presence. Very popular in formal tropical and Mediterranean-style Gulf Coast landscapes.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Yucca elephantipes (Spineless Yucca)
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Yucca species for Texas landscapes
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Yucca elephantipes
Royal Horticultural Society — Yucca elephantipes cultivation

common_name: Assam Indigo (Indigo Bush, Japanese Indigo Strobilanthes)

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial to full shade with consistently moist, humus-rich soil; Strobilanthes cusia prefers cool, shaded conditions and will perform poorly in full sun in zone 9. In zone 9a, it may die back to the roots in hard winters but resproutes vigorously in spring. Harvest leaves in summer (June-August) when indigo content is highest for natural dyeing. The blue-purple fall flowers appear September-December.

directions: Plant in partial to deep shade in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil; Strobilanthes cusia performs best in dappled shade and will wilt or scorch in full afternoon sun in zone 9. Strobilanthes cusia (Assam Indigo or Indigo Strobilanthes) is a subtropical shrub native to the hill country of Assam (India) and southern China that has been cultivated for centuries as the primary source of natural indigo dye in Japan, where it is called 'Ryukyu-ai' and used to produce the distinctive deep blue of traditional Japanese katazome (paste-resist dyeing) and aizome (indigo dyeing). The leaves contain the indigo precursor indican, which when fermented produces indigotin — the same deep blue pigment as in Indigofera tinctoria (true indigo), though the two plants are from different families. In zone 8–10 Gulf Coast and Southeast gardens, Strobilanthes cusia is a striking shade plant: large, dark-green, quilted leaves form a bold textural mass in shaded beds, and in fall (September–December) the plant produces compact clusters of small, violet-blue, snapdragon-like flowers. An excellent shade shrub with historical textile craft significance. The leaves can be processed into a paste indigo vat for natural dyeing wool, silk, and cotton.
sources: Ashford, J. (2011) The Weaver's Book of 8-Shaft Patterns — Natural indigo dye plant notes
Japanese Society for Vegetable Dye — Ryukyu-ai (Strobilanthes cusia) cultivation
World Agroforestry Center — Strobilanthes cusia traditional use
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Indigo Plants for Florida: Strobilanthes
